title: HST CCD Cosmic-Ray Rejection Benchmark
question: How do cosmic-ray rejection parameters trade detection recall against false masking and aperture-flux bias on HST CCD images?
gap: A controlled benchmark with injected ground truth; not a replacement for Astro-SCRAPPY, deepCR, Cosmic-CoNN or HST pipelines.
novelty: A controlled benchmark of astroscrappy's (L.A.Cosmic, van Dokkum 2001) published sigclip parameter against known-ground-truth synthetic cosmic-ray injections on real HST ACS/WFC background frames; not a new rejection algorithm and not a replacement for Astro-SCRAPPY, deepCR, Cosmic-CoNN, or the standard HST calibration pipeline.
validationContract: pixel-level precision/recall/F1 against exactly-known injected cosmic-ray pixels
PSF-core false-masking rate against DAOStarFinder/synthetic point sources
background masking rate (documented upper bound on the true false-positive rate)
aperture-flux fractional bias (photutils, local annulus background)
bootstrap 95% CIs (1000 resamples, seed 20260713) on recall/precision/flux bias
at least one null/negative-control test (CR-free field, low false-positive rate)
methodology: A small real ACS/WFC FLT (calibrated, uncombined single-exposure) cutout, or a clearly-labelled synthetic background for the demo path, serves as the background image. Known synthetic cosmic-ray tracks (short, sharp, non-PSF-convolved multi-pixel energy deposits, excluded from overlapping any detected point-source core) are injected at random positions with recorded exact pixel footprint and amplitude. astroscrappy.detect_cosmics (a published L.A.Cosmic implementation, van Dokkum 2001) is run at five sigclip values (3.0, 4.5, 6.0, 8.0, 10.0) with sigfrac=0.3 and objlim=5.0 held at their documented defaults, repeated over independent injection trials per setting. Detection quality is scored against the known truth (precision/recall/F1), against real/synthetic point-source cores (PSF-core false-masking rate), and against the rest of the frame (background masking rate, reported as an upper bound since a real frame may already contain unlabelled real cosmic rays). Aperture photometry (photutils, local annulus background) on the cleaned array versus the pre-injection background gives the fractional flux bias introduced by each sigclip setting.
assumptions: Archive metadata and calibration versions (CCDGAIN, READNSE*, EXPTIME, BUNIT) are recorded and interpreted correctly; calibrated ACS/WFC FLT arrays already in electrons are not double-converted by the detection gain.
Synthetic cosmic-ray tracks are a deliberately simple geometric line-track model (not a Monte Carlo particle-transport simulation), documented as such rather than presented as physically exhaustive.
sigfrac and objlim are held at astroscrappy's documented defaults throughout the sweep; only sigclip is varied.
Synthetic examples and injected cosmic rays are used only for validation, smoke testing, and controlled benchmarking, and are never presented as unmodified real observations.
limitations: The first release is intentionally bounded and does not replace mature mission or community pipelines (astroscrappy itself, deepCR, Cosmic-CoNN, or calacs).
Results apply only to the selected ACS/WFC product(s), the injected cosmic-ray amplitude/length range used, and the sigclip/sigfrac/objlim combinations swept here.

The background masking rate is an upper bound on the true false-positive rate: a real HST background frame may already contain unlabelled real cosmic-ray hits that astroscrappy correctly flags, which this metric cannot distinguish from a genuine false positive.
No causal claim is made from this descriptive parameter-sweep benchmark; no unsupported precision beyond the input uncertainties is reported.

citation.author: Biswajit Jana
